perturbation.original_text: The Company will provide to the NFLA-NC upon request the most recent quarterly sales report of the Company's Licensed Products.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may, at its sole discretion, provide sales reports to the NFLA-NC.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the mandatory provision of quarterly sales reports eliminates transparency and makes it difficult for the NFLA-NC to verify the accuracy of donation calculations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 6321 requires nonprofit public benefit corporations (like NFLA-NC) to maintain adequate and correct books and records of account and provide them to members upon request. By making the provision of sales reports discretionary, the company inhibits the ability of the NFLA-NC to maintain accurate records and potentially violates this section of the California Corporations Code, as the NFLA-NC needs sales reports to calculate royalty amounts.

Required Records, Reports to Directors and Members [6320 - 6325] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 1978, Ch. 567. ) 6321. (a) Except as provided in subdivision (c), (d), or (f), the board shall cause an annual report to be sent to the members not later than 120 days after the close of the corporation’s fiscal year. Unless otherwise provided by the articles or bylaws and if approved by the board of directors, that report and any accompanying material sent pursuant to this section may be sent by electronic transmission by the corporation (Section 20). That report shall contain in appropriate detail the following: (1) The assets and liabilities, including the trust funds, of the corporation as of the end of the fiscal year. (2) The principal changes in assets and liabilities, including trust funds, during the fiscal year. (3) The revenu
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated the provision of quarterly sales reports, ensuring transparency and enabling the NFLA-NC to verify donation calculations. The changed text makes the provision of these reports discretionary, potentially hindering the NFLA-NC's ability to maintain accurate records as required by California Corporations Code § 6321. The law snippet 1, referencing CORP 6321, discusses required records and reports. By removing the mandatory provision, the modified contract introduces a legal contradiction by making it harder for NFLA-NC to fulfill its legal obligations regarding record keeping, which is an omission legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Donated amounts will be allocated and dispersed to the Northern California Chapter beginning on the first full quarter [three (3) month period] of the Agreement and continue on a quarterly basis thereafter for the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Donated amounts may be allocated and dispersed to the Northern California Chapter at the Company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be' to 'may be' and adding 'at the Company's discretion' removes the obligation for timely and consistent charitable donations. This creates uncertainty and potential legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17510.8 requires that charitable solicitations clearly and unequivocally state the manner in which donations will be used. By making the allocation and dispersal of donated funds discretionary, the agreement becomes non-compliant with this code, because it no longer clearly dictates how the donations are used. The original text included specific dates for the disbursement, and the changed text removes this obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17510.8 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1.3. Charitable Solicitations [17510 - 17510.95] ( Article 1.3 added by Stats. 1972, Ch. 1113. ) 17510.8. Notwithstanding any other provision of this article, there exists a fiduciary relationship between a charity or any person soliciting on behalf of a charity, and the person from whom a charitable contribution is being solicited. The acceptance of charitable contributions by a charity or any person soliciting on behalf of a charity establishes a charitable trust and a duty on the part of the charity and the person soliciting on behalf of the charity to use those charitable contributions for the declared charitable purposes for which they are sought. This section is declarative of existing trust law principles. (Added by Stats. 1992, Ch. 1170, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17510.8. The original text mandated that donated amounts 'will be allocated' on a schedule, whereas the changed text makes this allocation discretionary ('may be allocated at the Company's discretion'). Snippet 1, referencing BPC 17510.8, establishes a 'fiduciary relationship' requiring charities to use contributions 'for the declared charitable purposes for which they are sought.' By removing the mandatory allocation and disbursement schedule, the changed text introduces ambiguity and undermines the fiduciary duty to use donations for their intended purpose, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: A *donation of $0.05 per Unit sold of Licensed Products within the Contract Territory payable to the **NFL Alumni Northern California Chapter.
perturbation.changed_text: A donation may be provided based on the company's profits.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies the amount of donation per unit sold. The modified text makes it dependent on company profits, which are easily manipulated and can remove the certainty of donations, contradicting charitable solicitation laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17510.3 requires that, in any solicitation or sales presentation for charitable purposes, it must be disclosed if the goods or services will benefit a charity. Furthermore, the percentage of the purchase price or the actual or estimated dollar amount of the purchase price that will benefit the charity has to be disclosed. The modified text removes the certainty of a specific amount ($0.05 per Unit) and makes it dependent on 'company profits,' which are highly variable, thus hindering compliance with the requirement for clear and unequivocal disclosure about what amount will benefit the charity.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1.3. Charitable Solicitations [17510 - 17510.95] ( Article 1.3 added by Stats. 1972, Ch. 1113. ) 17510.3. (a) Prior to any solicitation or sales solicitation for charitable purposes, the solicitor or seller shall exhibit to the prospective donor or purchaser a card entitled “Solicitation or Sale for Charitable Purposes Card.” The card shall be signed and dated under penalty of perjury by an individual who is a principal, staff member, or officer of the soliciting organization. The card shall give the name and address of the soliciting organization or the person who signed the card and the name and business address of the paid individual who is doing the actual soliciting. In lieu of exhibiting a card, the solicitor or seller may distribute during the course of the solicitation any printed material, such as a solicitation brochure, provided the material complies with the standards set forth below, and provided that the solicitor or seller informs th
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17510.3, as explained by the LLM, because it removes the certainty of a specific donation amount. The original text specified '$0.05 per Unit sold,' while the changed text makes donations dependent on 'company profits.' This undermines the requirement for clear disclosure about the amount that will benefit the charity, as stated in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which mentions the necessity of disclosing the 'percentage of the purchase price or the actual or estimated dollar amount of the purchase price that will benefit the charity.'
